title: Association between urban shrinkage and excess mortality during the COVID-19 pandemic

data_scope.key_effect_sizes: shrinking counties: +165% excess deaths, +142% mortality peaks vs growing counties

core_conclusion: Across 1,142 US counties during the COVID-19 pandemic, shrinking counties had 165% higher excess deaths and 142% more mortality peaks than growing counties; counties contracting in both population and GRDP fared worst, excess deaths rose with the severity of shrinkage, and socioeconomic disadvantages concentrated in shrinking places were linked to elevated mortality, motivating place-based public health strategies.
why_it_matters: Reframes urban shrinkage as a measurable public-health risk factor, distinguishes lethal compound contraction from single-axis decline, establishes a dose-response signal, and provides a reproducible evidence base for geographically targeted crisis response.
why_nature: Couples urban demographic-economic trajectory to human mortality at multi-county scale with a transferable typology; strongly policy-translatable for place-based health planning; timely structural lens on the pandemic; open public data plus released R/Python code.
added_value_to_my_research: Explainable ML (SHAP) + MGWR to recover local non-stationary drivers of county excess mortality
Insert 2SFCA/R2SFCA healthcare-access covariate as a mediator of shrinkage to mortality
Mobility-based causal decomposition of the economic-shrinkage axis via multi-source data fusion
Spatiotemporal graph / SIR-informed model of mortality peaks over the county commuting network
Generalize the four-type vulnerability signature to heat/cold and multi-hazard excess mortality
